Happy navigating!

Readiness@Scale 
SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Master Data Management: The Foundation for Modern Enterprise Data Architecture
entry.content:
Within an enterprise, critical data such as customer information, product details, asset records, and location data is used across various business units and operational systems. Managing this "master data" to ensure its accuracy, timeliness, and consistency is the core function of Master Data Management (MDM). This process, complemented by robust data governance practices, ensures that the responsibility for data quality is appropriately managed and verified. Over the past two decades, the software industry has provided systems to support MDM, but what functionalities are essential for a modern MDM system today?

Core Functions of a Modern MDM System

Data Integration and Cleansing

A modern MDM system should access data from operational systems and bring it together in a master data hub. This involves loading and synchronizing data, cleansing it to correct errors, and enriching records—for instance, filling missing postal codes, tax IDs or adding demographic information – leveraging data quality services, like SAP Data Quality Management, microservices for location data, or third party data providers, like CDQ (Corporate Data Quality).

Data Matching and Merging

With master data distributed across numerous systems, identifying duplicate records is crucial. The system should be capable of merging candidate records into a single, trusted master record (sometimes called the “best record” or “golden record”) through predefined rules based on criteria like how recently they were updated or their completeness and, sometimes, human intervention.

Data Governance and Workflow

A modern MDM system should support data governance processes, including access controls, data ownership, and audit trails. Automated, customizable workflow features for data stewards to manage data issues and approvals for changes are also essential. This allows various teams to own unique master data attributes and enforce validated values for specific data points through collaborative workflow routing and notification.

Hierarchy and Classification Management

Managing hierarchies of classification (e.g., legal business hierarchies, retail shop locations, customer classifications) is a key feature. The system should handle complex, unbalanced hierarchies efficiently, including the ability to retrieve legal hierarchies from external data providers like a Dun & Bradstreet so that the MDM system can load that data, solve any conflicts, and replicate the hierarchy information to consuming applications.

Analytics and Reporting

The system should provide intuitive analytics and reporting capabilities, highlighting data quality metrics and trends over time.  The MDM solution should allow data experts to define, validate, and monitor established business rules to confirm master data readiness and quality.  There should also be the ability analyze the performance of master data management processes (i.e. number of open master data change requests or the average time to close a change request).

Scalability and Deployment Options

A modern MDM system should be scalable and support both on-premise and cloud-based deployment options, adapting as source data migrates between different platforms.

Security and Usability

The system should have role-based security, ensuring only authorized users can perform specific tasks. An intuitive, customizable user interface and industry or data domain-specific templates can speed up deployment.

Supporting Current Initiatives

A robust MDM system supports digitalization, cloud migration, and business process automation. It also provides high-quality data for training AI models, making the AI more effective. As businesses increasingly adopt generative AI, having a reliable MDM system ensures that the AI models are trained on accurate, trustworthy data – and the most important data is your business-critical master data.

MDM and Data Fabric

The data fabric approach aims to provide business users direct access to corporate data assets without traditional data warehouses. However, the semantic layer of a data fabric must navigate numerous underlying operational systems to determine the most up-to-date and trustworthy data. An MDM system is instrumental here, as it carries out the critical work of cleaning, merging, and enriching data to create "golden records." By using master data from the MDM hub, the data fabric's semantic layer can navigate enterprise data assets more effectively, simplifying queries and providing accurate, consistent data for business users.

MDM as a Foundation for Generative AI

Generative AI, based on large language models (LLMs), has the potential to revolutionize various industries. However, LLMs frequently produce unreliable content due to poor-quality training data from publicly available sources. Corporations are now training LLMs on corporate-specific data for better accuracy.

MDM systems provide the high-quality, timely, and accurate data needed to train these LLMs effectively. Companies with well-managed MDM hubs have a competitive advantage, as their AI models perform significantly better with reliable data foundations.

Conclusion

Master data management remains a cornerstone of modern enterprise data architecture. Whether implementing a data fabric or training generative AI, having a well-managed master data hub is essential. The ability to access, clean, and enrich data ensures that enterprises can leverage advanced technologies effectively, driving innovation and maintaining a competitive edge. Investing in MDM is not just a safeguard for data quality; it is a strategic move that underpins numerous modern data initiatives.

Read the full paper on the capabilities and benefits of a modern master data management system here and listen to our podcast.

Learn more about SAP’s solution for master data management, SAP Master Data Governance, at www.sap.com/mdg.

entry.title: General Release of SAP Analytics Cloud compass
entry.content:
I can't be prouder to announce the upcoming general release of SAP Analytics Could compass, which is planned for 2025 Q1 (targeted fast track 2024.25). Finally, questions such as the one here can be answered via a few clicks, instead of multi-day discussions: "What is the impact on the operating income if the inflation, labour cost and gross revenue are not as planned?"

What is compass?

Compass is the new Monte Carlo simulation functionality, enabling the analysis of possible outcomes when faced with uncertainties in performance drivers. It can be used to explore probable range of company earnings, required workforce, sales target, ROI, project duration etc. ... the list is endless. With compass, we have broken down the barrier between this mathematical technique and business users by stripping away the complexity and providing an end-user centric interface for configuration and risk analysis. Now non-technical user can also enjoy the benefits of stochastic simulation without prior advanced maths knowledge or IT setup. No more coding, no data or formula duplication; just click-and-run! Functionalities will include:

Scenario Modelling and Comparison
Model "What-if" assumptions for multiple driver fluctuations and use scenarios to group them into separate concepts. This is particularly useful for the comparison of strategies or impact of uncontrollable events. If desired, chosen scenario outcomes can be displayed alongside to compare the chances and risks involved for each. 

Multidimensional Simulation
Configure possible performance fluctuations for different dimensions and combinations (e.g. products, regions, company codes, business units, period etc.), and simulate their compound effect on a dependent target. The flexible interface also allows for the changing of multidimensional drivers without any coding. This allows e.g. the quick integration of geographical / business segment aspects into the risk analysis, and offers transparency to the assumptions made at the same time.  

Insights to Pessimistic / Realistic / Optimistic cases
The impact distribution and risk context of each scenario is made comprehensible by a graphical representation of the pessimistic, realistic and optimistic cases, each including boundary values and chances in percentile. Elements such as the color, name or percentile of each case can be customized to add more context.  

Risk Context Exploration for a Specific Goal
With compass, user can choose to explore the entire impact distribution of driver uncertainties, or zoom in to explore the chances of achieving a specific goal. Questions such as "What are the chances of us achieving 50 m$ this year?" could be easily answered. Combined with the insights from the pessimistic, realistic and optimistic cases, users can quickly comprehend whether the goal is tending towards the aggressive or conservative edge, obtaining in turn an objective definition of the risk involved which could be checked against subjective interpretation.

Simulation Collaboration With Private and Public Scenarios
Within a compass simulation, the scenario created can be used for private experiments or be made public and disclosed to shared users. This enables simulation collaboration and traceability of assumptions made, down to individual level, if desired. The sharing rights are governed by the established file repository framework and come with similar flexibility and control as when sharing models and stories or any other SAP Analytics Cloud artifact. 

Simulation Persistency
Simulations can be saved in the file repository alongside models and other artifacts for reuse and administration. This way, not only collaborations are enabled, but also the benefit of concept traceability: Assumptions made in the past can be easily retrieved and compared to the actuals later for validation of simulation approach. This, if done along time, can help improving understanding of the data dynamic within own company. 

Driver Uncertainty Configuration
All possible drivers in the model for a chosen KPI will be automatically detected and simulation can be started right away. Simply enter the minimum and maximum values expected for a desired driver; these will be used as guidance for the impact calculation. Model the fluctuation behaviour by choosing between normal and uniform distribution type. 

On-the-spot activating / deactivating of effects
Single-click interaction to include or remove uncertainty effects for simulation, without remodelling scenario. This allows for agile on-the-spot experiments when working towards a conceptual grouping of assumptions. 

License

Compass is a native feature of SAP Analytics Cloud and is planned to be available with the planning standard license.

Join Our Journey

With the general release of compass, the first step is done towards the long term vision that some of you might already have seen in Data Unleashed 2024 .  While the general release will not come with AI integration, we strive to improve compass constantly and our vision still remains clear. We invite you to join us on our journey to push compass further by trying it out once it is generally available and let us know how it can help you even more by using the influence portal. 

Keep an eye on compass too with the Roadmap Explorer!

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in December
entry.content:
What a year it has been. Wow! 2024 was packed with great events, lots of new features and enhancements to extend existing features. Explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and enjoy watching my top feature highlights for 2024 on YouTube below.

 

SAP Datasphere 2024 highlights in a nutshell

What a great ride it has been in 2024 with SAP Datasphere!
Let me share a few of my personal highlights.

 

184 innovations were delivered according to What's New in SAP Datasphere on SAP Help. This is just the tip of the iceberg, and we know there is always a demand for more features and even quicker delivery. Thousands of code lines were written, tested, deployed and finally delivered as part of the solution. Kudos 👏 to all you developers, UX designers, fellow product managers to make this happen. You guys rock 💪

20k+ views of monthly top feature videos on YouTube about SAP Datasphere. I started this monthly series in January 2024 and did not expect this kind of success. Thanks for bearing with me and all the great feedback I got ❤️

11,967 attendees live at all SAP Datasphere sessions at SAP TechEd. This year we only had a virtual TechEd event. Personally, I love the onsite events and the interaction with customers, partners, consultants at our both and also during the lectures and hands-on sessions. Nevertheless, the studio interaction with my partners in crime Nikola Braukmüller and Dominik Kurz was lots of fun. Blessed to have such great teammates 🙏

 

My top features in 2024

Picking my top features for all of 2024 was not easy and here is my top feature list in no particular order.

Folder Support

In January we delivered the long-awaited support for folders inside the data builder and repository of SAP Datasphere. The feature allows to organize your content in folders within a space.

 

Nested & Shared Task Chains

February already covered my next favorite feature which are nested task chains, so the ability to use other task chains within a task chain. These meta chains help you coordinate many other task chains and since November you can share them across spaces to enable global task chains.

 

Confluent Kafka

Event streaming is a great way of data exchange between systems. Confluent is one of our open data ecosystem partners and this year we delivered Confluent Kafka as a target earlier in the year, and since November we also support Confluent Kafka as a source in replication flows to enable end-to-end scenarios.

 

Column Level Lineage

As a data modeler and data integrator it is always good to understand the flow of data on a field level. With the column level lineage, the impact and lineage analysis is now enabled to get one level deeper and allows you to select one or more columns in your analyzed object and show their sources and the columns that they impact.

 

Elastic Compute Nodes

The elastic compute nodes (ECN) enable you to get additional compute resources on-demand – while keeping your baseline compute resources untouched. You can spin up ECN’s according to your own schedule to help you expand elasticity during peak consumption scenarios and decommission them during regular operations.

 

Object store (Controlled availability)

With our final release of the year, we delivered the object store in a controlled availability. The object store provides an inbound layer to stage data in a cost-effective way. You can use replication flows to replicate data to local tables (file) and you can optionally further prepare the data with Apache Spark-based transformation flows. You can then share the tables to standard spaces, where they can be used as sources for flows, views, and analytic models (SAP Note 3525760, 3538038).

 

 

And more feature highlights of 2024

There are so many more great features which we delivered in 2024, so in addition to my 2024 top features, I pulled together the 2024 feature highlights in all different areas of SAP Datasphere for you. Enjoy the ride!

 

Data Modelling

 

Catalog & CLI

 

Data Integration

 

Spaces, Elasticity, Transport and more

 

My top blogs in December

This second blog in the SAP BW series by @SandyTran offers great value to our big community of BW experts looking for options on how to implement certain functions well known from SAP BW and BW/4HANA inside of SAP Datasphere. Especially interesting are the dynamic filtering options.
➡️  SAP Datasphere: BW Series – Getting Started (Part 2)

@Sefan_Lindersexplains how you can access two SAP Datasphere spaces using the same SQL user and gives you a step-by-step guide how you create such a user
➡️  Create a SQL user with access to multiple spaces in SAP Datasphere

 

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting. Also check out the new support content for SAP Datasphere on SAP Help for troubleshooting and analysis guides, how-to guides, technical details, and more.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: From History to Innovation: Analyzing Custom SAP System Evolution - A Primer
entry.content:
A Glimpse into the History of Custom SAP Systems

Consider the transport history of a small to medium-sized enterprise (SME). This record captures essential moments in the system's life - when transports occurred, who approved them, and which objects were moved into production. Such insights provide a clear picture of how the system has developed over time.

Example of ERP system workbench transport history (SME customer)Example of ERP system customizing transport history (same SME customer)

The volume of transported objects varies with company size. Larger enterprises often show a higher density of daily transports, reflecting more extensive system usage:

Another customer example of an SAP ECC workbench transport history. The blue hatched area represents the density of the daily transports as additional information 

It becomes more interesting when we take the transport history of a software package (DEVLASS) as an example for this customer and plot the individual developers and their activities:

An example software package (DEVLASS) workbench transport history of this customer

Each horizontal line represents the activity of a user within the software package (DEVLASS). The names of the users are grayed out for privacy reasons)

When looking at the activities based on the workbench transport history and considering the time-limited activities of users along with their simultaneous or staggered actions, we can discuss the development history and the developers involved. This makes for a good conversation starter with the relevant development manager, system administrator, or subject matter expert: "Which developer is the most experienced?", "Was knowledge able to be passed on between developers?", "Can I identify development initiatives?", "These two developers are our best procurement process experts!"

The way this information is presented is well-suited for storytelling.

 

Storytelling and Reconstructed History

Storytelling transforms complex technical processes into relatable narratives. Instead of drowning stakeholders in dry details, the narrative approach fosters collaboration between IT teams and business stakeholders. When technical information is presented as a story, IT professionals can communicate their ideas more effectively, leading to richer discussions about system features and enhancements.

However, storytelling comes with its challenges. One risk is exaggeration or misrepresentation; presenting hypothetical scenarios as facts can create misunderstandings and unrealistic expectations. To maintain trust, it’s crucial that stories are grounded in real data.

 

Understanding the Evolution of Custom SAP Systems for Better Transformation

Every custom SAP system tells a story shaped by organizational decisions throughout its development. This narrative encompasses not just technical specifications but also the experiences of those involved. Analyzing this evolution helps organizations make informed decisions about their software strategies.

Having spent years in Custom Development and Innovative Business Solutions (IBSO), I’ve seen firsthand how important it is to examine software evolution. The method of Software Evolution Analysis arose from the need for quick communication with clients about custom systems. This analysis captures the essence of a system's history, showing how past choices influence current functions and future developments.

In future blog posts, I will go deeper into this topic, sharing examples and insights that demonstrate how storytelling can clarify the complexities of custom SAP systems. Additionally, I will showcase analyses and modeling techniques that further enhance our understanding of these systems.

 

References

  1. Change and Transport System (CTS): https://support.sap.com/en/tools/software-logistics-tools/change-and-transport-system.html 
  2. Lehman, M. M., & Belady, L. (1974). "Program Evolution: Processes of Software Change."  (remark: The article by Lehman was published in 1974, just two years after the founding of SAP in 1972 by former IBM employees.); Lehman, M. M. (1980). "Programs, Life Cycles, and the Laws of Software Evolution." In Proceedings of the IEEE.
  3. Create Meaning by Storytelling: Fisher, Walter R. (1985). The Narrative Paradigm: An Elaboration. Communication Monographs, 51(4), 347-67. https://doi.org/10.1080/03637758509376117; Rooney, T., Lawlor, K., & Rohan, E. (2016). Telling Tales: Storytelling as a Methodological Approach in Research. The Electronic Journal of Business Research Methods, 14(2), 147-156. https://academic-publishing.org/index.php/ejbrm/article/download/1349/1312/1345

entry.title: Shaping the Future: SAP Analytics Cloud as a Resilient Force in the AI-Driven Evolution of Business
entry.content:
The Artificial Intelligence Storm

In today’s rapidly evolving technological world, businesses face the dual challenge of staying competitive while navigating through disruptive trends like artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML). Many platforms face the risk of obsolescence as they struggle to keep pace with changing demands. Nevertheless, despite these transformation trends, SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) continues to prove its relevance by adapting and integrating into a dynamic ecosystem and landscapes of the companies and benefits from a continuous commitment to improvement and adoption. SAC remains a vital tool for organizations that are seeking to derive value from their data, as it is core to SAP’s product portfolio focusing on driving future innovation.

Evolving with the AI Revolution

SAC’s Integration and Privileges in the AI Storm

AI has fundamentally shifted the way organizations approach business intelligence (BI) and analytics. Traditional dashboards and manual reports are being enhanced or even fully replaced by predictive and augmented analytics and conversational BI tools. Companies are increasingly demanding platforms capable not only of analyzing historical data but also of anticipating trends and suggesting actionable insights in real time.

SAP Analytics Cloud is not just adapting to these demands but thrives to represent such an environment itself. It embeds machine learning and AI-driven analytics directly into its core functionalities and as a result, empowers its users to go beyond descriptive analytics. For example, its predictive planning capabilities allow businesses to simulate scenarios and to plan for outcomes with the highest accuracy. SAC supports other wide range of functionalities, such as "augmented analytics," which enables  AI to guide users through complex datasets and highlight patterns

Apart from the functionalities, one of SAP Analytics Cloud’s strongest assets is its seamless integration into SAP’s product ecosystem. Businesses that are already leveraging SAP solutions—be it SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, or SAP Ariba—find that SAC fits naturally into their workflows. This integration ensures a unified data landscape where users can access real-time insights without additional challenges or fragmented datasets.

SAC acts as a bridge between on-premises and cloud environments, allowing businesses to adopt hybrid analytics strategies. This flexibility is particularly critical for companies in industries with strict compliance requirements or those undergoing cloud transformations.

By remaining deeply embedded within the SAP portfolio, SAC reinforces its relevance. As organizations adopt new AI-driven tools and methodologies, SAC’s ability to integrate with emerging technologies ensures it evolves in parallel with the broader business environment.

Furthermore, SAP’s roadmap for SAC emphasizes agility, with regular updates and new features designed to align with emerging trends. This ensures that customers not only have access to state-of-the-art analytics but also a sense of security that their investment will remain relevant in the years to come. SAC is a dynamic platform, built to evolve and adjust in harmony with its users' needs.

Looking Ahead

The future of analytics is undeniably intertwined with Artificial Intelligence. Yet, the need for robust, reliable, and integrated BI tools remains unchanged. SAP Analytics Cloud exemplifies how a platform can adapt to technological shifts while remaining a dependable solution for businesses.

As the AI era progresses, SAC will continue to evolve, delivering actionable insights, fostering collaboration, and driving business outcomes. For organizations seeking a future-proof BI solution, SAC’s deep integration with SAP products and commitment to innovation makes it a natural choice

By focusing on integration, usability, and technological evolution, SAP Analytics Cloud is poised to remain at the forefront of analytics innovation, ensuring that businesses can thrive in an ever-changing world.

 

Andrii Pogorielov, Seda Agasarjan

Customer Services & Delivery MEE BT&A CFO Advisory

entry.title: Consuming Data from Databricks to Datasphere
entry.content:
Prerequisites:

  • Access: Access to both SAP Datasphere and Databricks.
  • Credentials: Have Databricks authentication credentials ready.

Steps:

  1. Login to Datasphere:
  •  In the home screen of the datasphere, Expand the navigation bar on the left and click “Configuration”.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  1. Database Access
  • Click database access and then click Database Analysis Users

 

  • Then click “Create” to create a database access user

 

 

 

 

 

  • Enter the Database Analysis username suffix
  • Enable Space Schema Access
  • Set Database Analysis user expires in Never option.

 

 

  • Once created it will generate a Hostname, Port Number and Password and make sure that Enable Space schema access has been enabled, so by this you can be able to access Datasphere’s all spaces and its schemas

 

 

  1. Login to Databricks
  • Go to Workspace, create Cluster

 

 

  • In the cluster, paste this code to connect to datasphere and Databricks

Python

import org.apache.spark.sql.SparkSession

 

# Initialize Spark Session

spark = SparkSession.builder()\

    .appName("SAP HANA Connection")\

    .config("spark.jars", "/usr/sap/hdbclient/ngdbc-2.4.62.jar")\

    .getOrCreate()

 

# JDBC URL

jdbcUrl = "jdbc:sap://zeus.hana.prod.eu-central-1.whatever.cloud.sap:443/?encrypt=true&validateCertificate=false&currentschema=MYSCHEMA"

 

# Connection Properties

connectionProperties = {

  "user" : "MYUSER",

  "password" : "mypassword",

  "driver" : "com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver"

}

 

# Query to Select all the Data from MYTABLE

val query = "SELECT * FROM MYTABLE"

 

# Read Data from JDBC Connection into DataFrame

val df = spark.read.jdbc(jdbcUrl, query, connectionProperties)

 

 

  • Once code is executed successfully, the connection is established between the Datasphere and Databricks.

entry.title: Consuming Data from Databricks to SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Prerequisites 

Either ONE of the following setup options needs to be completed: 

Performed setup using the SAP Analytics Cloud Agent Simple Deployment Kit.  

 

For more information, see SAP Analytics Cloud Agent Simple Deployment Kit. 

Note 

JDBC drivers must be installed using the instructions in the Post-Setup Guide included in the kit. 

 

OR 

 

Perform a manual setup of the following required components: 

  • The Cloud Connector is installed. For more information, see Installing the Cloud Connector. 
    • The SAP Analytics Cloud agent is installed. For more information, see Installing SAP Analytics Cloud Agent. 
      • JDBC drivers must be installed. 
        • The Cloud Connector is configured. For more information, see Configuring the Cloud Connector. 
          • The SAP Analytics Cloud agent is configured in SAP Analytics Cloud. For more information, see Configuring SAP Analytics Cloud Agent. 
            • You have installed a JDBC driver. If you haven't already installed a JDBC driver, follow steps : Import Data Connection to an SQL Database | SAP Help Portal 
              • You must restart the SAP Analytics Cloud agent, using either the Java option -DSAP_CLOUD_AGENT_PROPERTIES_PATH (if the agent is installed on Linux), or an environment variable SAP_CLOUD_AGENT_PROPERTIES_PATH (if the agent is installed on Windows), to specify the complete path up to and including the properties file. Example path: C:\<full path to file>\DriverConfig.properties. Refer: Import Data Connection to an SQL Database | SAP Help Portal 

                Procedure 

                • In the Modeler app, while creating a new model or importing data into an existing SQL Database model, choose Get from a Data Source or Data source. 
                  • Select SQL Databases. 

                     

                     

                    The Create Model from SQL Databases dialog will appear.

entry.title: Re-initializing of deltas in BW4HANA from S4HANA (For error Subscription for DTP* deleted in source)
entry.content:
Overview:

Re-initializing deltas between S4HANA and BW4HANA is vital for maintaining precise data synchronization. This process entails removing the existing initialization request in BW4HANA, purging previous data from the data targets, and establishing a new initialization. The error message indicates that the subscription for the Data Transfer Process (DTP) has been removed in the source system (S4HANA).

To address this issue, examine the DTP settings in BW4HANA, recreate the subscription if necessary, review system logs for further details, and re-initialize the delta process.

Error Occurred in BW4HANA System:

Subscription for DTP* deleted in source system. The error is due to deletion on subscription in S4 side or the subscription of DTP is mapped to wrong source system.

 

Check ODQMON by putting data source name in queue and see the subscriptions. 

 

Solution to the error:

Step 1) To delete a subscription in T-Code ODQMON in S4HANA, open ODQMON, select the DataSource, go to the "Subscriptions" tab, and delete the desired subscription. Confirm the deletion by checking the "Queues" tab to ensure the request is inactive. 

 

Step 2) To initiate a new subscription from BW4HANA to S4HANA, it is essential to delete all existing requests in the target system. If these requests are not removed, you will encounter errors. Therefore, ensure that all pending requests are cleared before proceeding with the new subscription setup. This step is crucial to avoid any interruptions or issues during the process.

Open the monitor in SAP GUI

Change overall Status to RED 

And delete the requests one by one. Please ensure all the requests are deleted from the target before moving to next step.

Step 3) To begin, manually initiate the Data Transfer Process (DTP) in delta mode using Eclipse. After starting the DTP, check the ODQMON transaction in your S4HANA system to confirm that a new subscription has been created. This verification step is crucial to ensure that the data transfer setup is functioning correctly.

 

Note** If you continue to encounter errors on the BW4HANA side after completing the previous steps, please run the report RODPS_REPL_TEST in transaction SE38 for the data source on the S4HANA side. This report will help diagnose and resolve any remaining issues with the data transfer process.

entry.title: New Amazon S3 (Iceberg) Tables made us drop our Parquet library in ABAP
entry.content:
We have been developing the Parquet library in SAP ABAP for a while, not an easy thing to do. The problem we were trying to solve, integrating SAP with multiple Data Lakes is like re inventing the wheel over and over again, once we finish with one customer, we go into another, with different table requirements, data and metadata requirements, frequency, formats, and schemas. We need an output format with structure and metadata, and Parquet, ORC and Avro is our way out of the trap, until we reach higher Open standard goals, like Iceberg.

Currently, we use CSV and JSON file formats, both offered as standard ABAP libraries, provided by SAP, but customers ask for more. CSV and JSON are sometimes too basic for large enterprise, even Avro falls short, its great for streaming, but when you have billions of files, you got to scan entirely the files in CSV, JSON or Avro, and thats a lot of compute, then Parquet or ORC are a great fit, that is the problem they came to solve. In our case, Parquet was our only viable option because, at the time, Amazon Athena only supported Parquet, not ORC, not Avro, this changed recently, so we have gone all in on Parquet.

Parquet is a file format. It operates fundamentally as a columnar file format and focuses on efficient data storage and compression. So, seems like a great fit!

 

by Author

 

 All rainbows and unicorns… Parquet is fantastic for all the features we need for our SAP data integration, but we miss one, and its quite important one; Writing Parquet or ORC, is really hard. Only a lucky few tools like Databricks, Snowflake, Spark, Hive, Flink, have good support for writing Iceberg tables.

Writing an Iceberg table in any of the file formats requires much more care. Each write to an Iceberg table creates new data files in S3. Over time, the table’s data gets split across many small files, and reading the data from the table becomes prohibitively slow. The solution is to periodically compact the table, which combines the data files into fewer larger files and drops any data that has been overwritten/deleted. See this article from Dremio for a better explanation.

Not to mention, the Python Iceberg library nor the Rust Iceberg library today support compaction, that also had to be built on ABAP. Only Java is an option for built libraries, or we build ourselves. Mountain high, Valley Low.

So, Parquet structure is like a ledger of changes, all changes are added as snapshots to the original state.

 

day 2 with Parquet

On the SAP world this was a problem for us, given the nature of changes in SAP data and the rate of updates, we were quickly with hundreds of thousands of objects under every table.

 

Each daily update will create Parquet files, partitioned by GJAHR and BUKRSas an example

 

 

s3://bucket/warehouse/acdoca_table/
  ├── GJAHR=2024/
      ├── BUKRS=1000/
          ├── part-00001.parquet
          ├── part-00002.parquet
      ├── BUKRS=2000/
          ├── part-00003.parquet
...
...

 

 

 

However, as data usage patterns evolved, a need for more dynamic capabilities was needed. While Parquet can be used for mutable tables (e.g., by appending new files), this often involves manual management and can lead to inefficiencies, especially in scenarios with high write volumes or complex data evolution.

S3 table is a bucket with Iceberg table format that creates a rest endpoint on a per-table basis. Inside that bucket we get an Iceberg catalog, we can create namespaces and tables, each table is a first-class resource, and AWS runs all the maintenance and optimization tasks of it.

by Author

 

So, Mario, this means that we are bringing the Catalog to the Storage Layer?

Or, scientifically asked are we shifting towards bringing database-like functionality and efficiency to the storage layer?

Light Weight!

 

 

In fact, we have been doing it, for a while, with Glue, but not from SAP directly. And we have been asked multiple times. Yet we had to rely on Glue.

AWS Glue cost structure is based on a pay-as-you-go model, which, while offering tremendous flexibility, also has some complexity and requires careful understanding. You got to understand DPUs, Glue Data Catalog and Crawlers to find and organize data for your AWS Glue Data Catalog, charged by the hour.

Resource overprovisioning, Over-allocation of memory and CPU resources, not utilizing on-demand ETL jobs effectively, inefficient partitioning of data, overutilization of AWS Glue crawlers, not using the job bookmark feature and data reprocessing or inefficient use of data formats like CSV or JSON instead of Parquet can increase the cost significantly and kill the project.

Pricing will kill the idea?

Before we activate the S3 table buckets, we receive a warning, we must enable integration.

 

by Author

 

This integration with AWS analytics services will integrate our table buckets with AWS analytics services. The integration adds our tables to the AWS Glue Data Catalog so we can work with them using analytics services such as Amazon Athena, Amazon Redshift or Amazon QuickSight.

 

 As you see, the integration is done per Region, once activated, for every new table bucket in the console, S3 initiates some actions to integrate table buckets in the Region. Lake Formation registers the table bucket in the current Region and adds the s3tablescatalog to the AWS Glue Data Catalog in the current Region.

All the table buckets, namespaces, and tables will be populated in the Data Catalog of Glue.

This is much more efficient than us managing Glue ourselves. We save significant data discovery and management burden, it helps us to reduce the need for manual metadata handling and minimizes the risk of errors that could lead to increased costs.

Natively integrating S3 tables with AWS Glue Data Catalog and AWS Lake Formation we mitigate several cost traps associated with AWS Glue, such as overutilization of crawlers, inefficient use of data formats, and overlooked partitioning of data.

How are we doing all this?

Table Operation in S3 Tables is quite similar to our current CRUD operation, we now must make sure that we can

 

 

 A set of APIs for interacting with our tables

 

  • Create Table: Create new tables within our table bucket.
  • List Tables: Discover all tables residing within a specific table bucket.
  • Read Table Metadata: Retrieve the latest Iceberg metadata file, essential for accessing the most up-to-date view of our data.
  • Direct Commits as CRUD (Create, Read,, Update and Delete) to commit changes to our tables directly using the provided APIs.

Table Management

  • Bucket-Level and Table-Level Control: Manage resources at both the table bucket and individual table levels.
  • Resource Policies: Implement fine-grained access control through resource policies.
  • Table Maintenance Policies: Fine-tune table maintenance behavior:
  • Compaction: Define target file sizes for efficient data compaction.
  • Snapshots: Control snapshot retention periods and automate their aging-out process.
by Author

 

 

  1. Metadata File is a JSON file that contains the latest version of a table. Any changes made to a table create a new metadata file. The contents of this file are simply lists of manifest list files with some high level metadata.
  2. Manifest File is the final step for a query as only files that need to be read are determined using these files saving valuable querying time.
  3. Manifest List of manifest files that make up a snapshot. This also includes metadata such as partition bounds in order to skip files that do not need to be read for the query.
  4. Table directory is the name of the table with a unique uuid in order to support table renames
  5. Namespace holds Parquet and JSON file formats.

Iceberg catalogs is key element as it keep track of which Iceberg tables live at which path in the S3 bucket and which files within that path belong to the live version of the table. Catalog ensures that two systems attempting to write to the table at the same time don’t corrupt each other’s writes. Reading from an Iceberg table doesn’t require interacting with the catalog, but writing to an Iceberg table always does.

Glue Data Catalog automatically populates table buckets, namespaces, and tables in the current Region as corresponding objects in the Data Catalog. Table buckets are populated as sub-catalogs. Namespaces within a table bucket are populated as databases within their respective sub-catalogs. Tables are populated as tables in their respective databases.

source; AWS

One of the biggest advantages of S3 Tables is its built-in catalog, aptly named S3TablesCatalog. The source code is available on GitHub for transparency, and it acts as a wrapper around the S3 Tables API. This means most Iceberg catalog operations directly translate to S3 Tables API calls.

For us as an Iceberg writer, this has been the game-changer. No more wrestling with choosing the right catalog implementation or running a separate service.

Important Performance indications

So, if we are going to use S3 for CRUD operations, we need a better performance. Compared to regular buckets, S3 Tables offer significantly improved performance compared to storing tabular data in general-purpose buckets. Up to 10 times higher transactions per second (TPS) out-of-the-box, translating to 55,000 reads/second and 35,000 writes/second. This performance boost is further amplified by automated background compaction, resulting in up to 3 times faster query performance compared to the previous option for Iceberg tables. Also, S3 automatically scales request capacity as our traffic demands grow, ensuring our applications always have the resources they need.

 

One of the biggest concerns on Iceberg is how table performance can degrade over time as data accumulates and more files are added. This leads to increased query latency due to the need to read from numerous small files.

Compaction is a crucial feature that addresses this issue by merging smaller data files into larger ones, automatically. This reduces the number of files to read, significantly improving query performance where peviously, we had to manually manage compaction processes.

Amazon just released a blog where they explain these optimizations if you are interested in reading.

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/storage/how-amazon-ads-uses-iceberg-optimizations-to-accelerate-their-spark-workload-on-amazon-s3/

 

Cost Optimizations

Storage Cost Optimization with Automated Snapshot Management

Iceberg maintains a history of changes through snapshots, allowing for time travel and rollbacks. However, accumulating numerous snapshots significantly increase storage costs. Previously, managing snapshot retention and cleaning up associated files required manual effort with Snapshot Expiration and identifying and deleting unreferenced files associated with expired snapshots.

S3 Tables automate these processes with an automated Snapshot Expiration. Policies allow us to specify the number of snapshots to retain and their retention duration. S3 Tables automatically expire snapshots according to these policies;

 

by Author

 

Storage Costs: The primary cost component, at $27.14/month for 1 TB of data.

 

Request Costs like PUT requests incur minimal costs, even with high-frequency daily ingestion. GET requests remain affordable for moderate query workloads.

Monitoring Costs: A small, predictable cost based on object count.

Compaction Costs: Object processing costs are negligible while Data processing during compaction adds significant cost ($7.50/month).

Frequent PUT/GET Requests: Real-time ingestion and querying could increase costs significantly.

Compaction Overheads: High-frequency small writes can drive up compaction costs, especially in real-time use cases.

What comes next for us?

Although this is exciting, there are some aspects of Amazon S3 Tables and its integration with other AWS services are still in public preview. This means that these features are available for testing and feedback, but may not be fully production-ready or may have some limitations.

Glue Data Catalog Integration: While S3 Tables automatically register with the Glue Data Catalog, creating tables directly through engines like Amazon Athena or Redshift is not yet supported. In our case, we are creating the tables directly from SAP using SQL queries.

 

 

CREATE TABLE sap_materials (
    material_id STRING,
    material_type STRING,
    description STRING,
    base_unit STRING,
    created_at TIMESTAMP,
    modified_at TIMESTAMP,
    storage_location STRING,
    batch_number STRING,
    quantity DECIMAL(15,3),
    value DECIMAL(15,2)
)
USING iceberg

 

 

 

 

S3 Metadata will provide a significant improvement from our SAP operations, the automatic generation of metadata for S3 objects and its storage in an S3 Table bucket is also in preview but certainly will bring us additional benefits for unstructured data

Databricks and Snowflake customers on AWS may see a low entry barrier for Iceberg, a competitor to Delta Lake. Iceberg was developed by Netflix as a replacement for Hive, and is managed by Apache foundation, Delta Lake by the Linux foundation. They are very similar, though different, and until now the barrier was on mostly the compute engine used. Lets see if in the future Amazon S3 will also support Delta Lake table format natively.

S3 Tables will drive our future innovation for Data Lake generation. It opens a world of possibilities in innovation in query engines and optimization strategies and provides new cost models to account for real-time workloads and high-frequency operations integration.

Ifyou want to know more, check out this document

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/apache-iceberg-on-aws/data-lakes.html

 

And, if you want to join this racket, this will help (easy to find online by Dremio)

https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/apache-iceberg-the/9781098148614/
